title: Advanced Git (Rebase, Cherry-pick, Stash)
summary: Level up your Git skills with advanced techniques. Learn interactive rebase for clean history, cherry-pick for selective commits, stash for saving work in progress, and how to recover from mistakes with reflog.

resources.title: Pro Git Book - Chapter 7: Git Tools
Git Rebase vs Merge - Which Should You Use?

questions.questionText: What does 'git rebase main' do when on a feature branch?
questions.explanation: Rebase replays the commits from your feature branch on top of the latest main branch. This creates a linear history without merge commits, as if you had started your work from the latest main.

options.text: Merges main into the feature branch
Replays feature branch commits on top of main
Creates a backup of the feature branch

questions.explanation: 'git stash' temporarily saves your uncommitted changes (both staged and unstaged) and reverts your working directory to the last commit. Use 'git stash pop' to restore them.

options.text: Saves uncommitted changes temporarily so you can switch branches
Commits all changes with a default message
Creates a new branch from current changes

questions.explanation: Cherry-pick applies the changes from a specific commit onto your current branch. It creates a new commit with the same changes but a different hash. Useful for backporting fixes.

options.text: Moves a commit from one branch to another
Applies the changes from a specific commit to the current branch

questions.explanation: Never rebase commits that have been pushed to a public/shared branch. Rebasing rewrites commit history, which causes problems for others who have based work on those commits.

options.text: On local feature branches before pushing
On commits that have been pushed to a shared branch
When cleaning up local commit history

tasks.title: Practice Rebase and Stash
tasks.description:
1. Create a new repo or use an existing one
2. Make 3 commits on main
3. Create a feature branch and make 2 commits
4. Go back to main and make 1 more commit (so the branches diverge)
5. Switch to the feature branch and rebase: `git rebase main`
6. View the log: `git log --oneline --graph --all`
7. Now practice stash:
   - Make changes to a file but do not commit
   - Run `git stash`
   - Verify working directory is clean: `git status`
   - Switch branches, do some work, switch back
   - Restore changes: `git stash pop`
8. List stashes: `git stash list`
9. Try `git stash save 'work in progress on feature X'` for labeled stashes
hints: Use 'git stash apply' instead of 'pop' to keep the stash entry
If rebase has conflicts, resolve them, then: git add <file> && git rebase --continue

tasks.title: Cherry-pick and Recovery with Reflog
tasks.description:
1. Create two branches with different commits
2. Use `git log --oneline` to find a specific commit hash on one branch
3. Switch to the other branch and cherry-pick it: `git cherry-pick <hash>`
4. Verify the commit was applied: `git log --oneline`
5. Now practice recovery:
   - Make a commit, then reset it: `git reset --hard HEAD~1`
   - The commit seems gone! View the reflog: `git reflog`
   - Find the lost commit hash in the reflog
   - Recover it: `git cherry-pick <hash>` or `git reset --hard <hash>`
6. Explore reflog: `git reflog show --all`
7. Understand that reflog is your safety net — Git rarely truly loses data
hints: Reflog entries expire after 90 days by default, so recovery is time-limited
git cherry-pick -n <hash> applies changes without committing, letting you modify before committing
